event: The Arrest of Christ — Judas's betrayal kiss in Gethsemane
symbols: Judas's kiss — the ultimate betrayal, intimacy perverted into treachery
armored soldiers — the worldly power arrayed against the innocent Christ
deep darkness — the hour of the power of darkness, as Jesus himself described it
fleeing disciple at left — the scattering of the sheep when the shepherd is struck
Christ's clasped hands — patient submission to the Father's will even in the moment of arrest
gleaming armor — the military force that cannot truly contain the divine
description: A dramatic Baroque painting showing a tight, compressed group of figures in a nocturnal scene. At the center, a bearded man (Judas) leans in closely toward Christ, who appears calm with downcast eyes and clasped hands. Christ wears a blue robe with a red cloak. Armored soldiers in gleaming metal press in from the right, one reaching to seize Christ. At the far left, a figure with an anguished expression recoils or flees with raised hands. The scene is lit by a single light source creating extreme chiaroscuro, with most of the background in total darkness.
theological_description: Caravaggio's Taking of Christ captures the most intimate moment of betrayal in Scripture — the kiss by which Judas identified Jesus to the arresting party (Matthew 26:48-49). Jesus had foretold this at the Last Supper: 'One of you will betray me' (Matthew 26:21), and Judas approached with the prearranged signal: 'The one I will kiss is the man; seize him' (Matthew 26:48). The use of a kiss — an act of love and greeting — as the instrument of betrayal makes this the supreme perversion of intimacy. Jesus's calm submission amidst the violence embodies Isaiah 53:7: 'He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth.' His clasped hands suggest the prayer of Gethsemane still fresh: 'Not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42). The fleeing figure at the left likely represents the disciples scattering, fulfilling Jesus's prophecy quoting Zechariah 13:7: 'Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered' (Matthew 26:31). Jesus himself identified the theological dimension of this moment: 'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53). Yet even here, the Gospel reveals that these events occur within God's sovereign plan: Christ was 'delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God' (Acts 2:23).

scripture_refs.reason: Judas's betrayal kiss and the arrest of Jesus — the event directly depicted
This is your hour and the power of darkness — the theological characterization of this nocturnal arrest
Strike the shepherd and the sheep will scatter — the fleeing disciple fulfilling prophecy
He did not open his mouth — Christ's calm submission before his captors
John's account of the arrest in the garden, including the detail of soldiers with lanterns
Delivered according to God's definite plan — the arrest as part of sovereign divine purpose
